class_schedule.topic: The International Economic Order: History and Structure
learning_objectives: Trace the evolution of the international economic order from Bretton Woods to the present
Identify the roles of the IMF, World Bank, and WTO
Understand the tension between free trade and protectionism

lecture_notes.introduction: The global economy does not operate in a political vacuum. International economic institutions reflect the power and preferences of the states that created them.
key_concepts: Bretton Woods system: fixed exchange rates, IMF, World Bank
Collapse of Bretton Woods and the move to floating exchange rates
GATT to WTO: the evolution of the trade regime
Washington Consensus: liberalization, privatization, stabilization
Critiques: dependency theory, world-systems theory, anti-globalization
lecture_notes.demonstration: Timeline exercise: key moments in international economic governance from 1944 to present

review.value: The post-WWII international monetary order (1944-1971) that pegged currencies to the dollar, which was convertible to gold, creating stability for trade expansion
A set of economic policy prescriptions (fiscal discipline, trade liberalization, privatization) promoted by the IMF and World Bank for developing countries in the 1980s-90s

learning_objectives: Explain comparative advantage and its implications for trade policy
Analyze the distributional effects of trade within countries
Evaluate contemporary trade disputes and protectionist pressures

lecture_notes.introduction: Trade theory says free trade makes countries richer. Trade politics shows that the gains and losses are unevenly distributed, creating powerful political conflicts.
key_concepts: Absolute advantage (Smith) vs. comparative advantage (Ricardo)
Heckscher-Ohlin model: factor endowments and trade patterns
Stolper-Samuelson theorem: trade's distributional effects
Strategic trade theory: when government intervention can help
Contemporary issues: trade wars, supply chain resilience, reshoring
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulation: two-country, two-good trade model demonstrating comparative advantage gains

review.value: Ricardo's principle that countries benefit from trade by specializing in goods they produce at lower opportunity cost, even if one country is more efficient at producing everything
Trade benefits the abundant factor of production and hurts the scarce factor, explaining why trade creates winners and losers within countries

learning_objectives: Explain how exchange rate regimes work and their trade-offs
Analyze the causes and consequences of financial crises
Evaluate the role of the IMF in crisis management

lecture_notes.introduction: International finance is the nervous system of the global economy. When it works, trade flourishes. When it fails, crises devastate entire regions.
key_concepts: Exchange rate regimes: fixed, floating, managed float
The impossible trinity: exchange rate stability, capital mobility, monetary autonomy
Balance of payments: current account and capital account
Financial crises: currency, banking, and sovereign debt crises
IMF conditionality: structural adjustment and its critics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study: the 1997 Asian Financial Crisis -- causes, contagion, and IMF response

review.value: The Mundell-Fleming trilemma: a country cannot simultaneously maintain a fixed exchange rate, free capital movement, and independent monetary policy -- it must sacrifice one
Policy reforms (austerity, liberalization, privatization) that the IMF requires in exchange for financial assistance, criticized for deepening recessions and imposing political costs

class_schedule.topic: Multinational Corporations and Global Value Chains
learning_objectives: Analyze the role of MNCs in the global economy
Explain how global value chains restructure production
Evaluate debates over FDI: benefits, costs, and regulation
readings: Gereffi, 'Global Value Chains in a Post-Washington Consensus World'

lecture_notes.introduction: Multinational corporations are among the most powerful actors in the global economy. Their investment decisions shape development trajectories for entire countries.
key_concepts: Foreign direct investment: horizontal vs. vertical FDI
Global value chains: fragmentation of production across borders
Race to the bottom vs. climb to the top in attracting FDI
Corporate social responsibility and supply chain governance
Tax havens, transfer pricing, and regulatory arbitrage
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping the global value chain of an iPhone: design, components, assembly, distribution

review.value: The full range of activities that firms perform to bring a product from conception to end use, now fragmented across multiple countries based on cost and capability
The concern that countries competing for FDI will lower labor, environmental, and tax standards, though evidence suggests this is more nuanced than critics claim

class_schedule.topic: Development Economics and the Global South
learning_objectives: Compare competing approaches to economic development
Analyze the effectiveness of foreign aid
Evaluate the rise of emerging economies and South-South cooperation
readings: Easterly, The White Man's Burden Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Why do some countries develop while others remain trapped in poverty? Development economics wrestles with this question, and the answers have enormous policy consequences.
key_concepts: Import substitution industrialization vs. export-oriented growth
The East Asian miracle: state-led development
Foreign aid: the debate between Sachs and Easterly
Microfinance and bottom-up development approaches
BRICS and the rise of South-South economic cooperation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative case study: South Korea and Nigeria -- similar GDP in 1960, divergent paths

review.value: Development strategy emphasizing production for global markets, associated with the East Asian tigers (South Korea, Taiwan, Singapore, Hong Kong)
Sachs argues massive aid investment can break poverty traps; Easterly argues top-down aid fails due to poor incentives and that bottom-up approaches work better

class_schedule.topic: The Future of the Global Economy: Challenges and Disruptions
learning_objectives: Analyze the impact of technology on global economic structures
Evaluate the tension between economic nationalism and globalism
Assess the economic dimensions of climate change

lecture_notes.introduction: The global economy faces unprecedented disruptions: technological revolution, climate crisis, geopolitical fragmentation, and rising inequality. How we respond will shape the next century.
key_concepts: Automation, AI, and the future of work globally
Digital currencies and the future of the monetary system
Economic nationalism and decoupling: US-China tech competition
Climate economics: carbon pricing, green transition, just transition
Global inequality and the social contract
lecture_notes.demonstration: Scenario planning exercise: three possible global economic orders in 2040
setup_requirements: Trend data on technology, trade, and climate

review.value: The prioritization of domestic economic interests over global integration, manifested in tariffs, industrial policy, and restrictions on technology transfer
The principle that the shift to a green economy must be managed so that the costs do not fall disproportionately on workers and communities dependent on fossil fuels

questions.question: Explain the theory of comparative advantage using a simple two-country, two-good example. Why does trade benefit both countries even if one country is more efficient at producing both goods?
questions.answer: Comparative advantage means each country should specialize in producing the good for which it has the lowest opportunity cost. Even if Country A produces both wine and cloth more efficiently than Country B (absolute advantage in both), trade still benefits both if A has a lower opportunity cost in wine and B has a lower opportunity cost in cloth. By specializing and trading, total output increases and both countries consume beyond their individual production possibilities. The key insight is that trade is driven by relative efficiency (opportunity cost), not absolute efficiency.

questions.question: What is the 'impossible trinity' in international finance? Explain why a country cannot achieve all three objectives simultaneously and give an example of a country that sacrificed one.
questions.answer: The impossible trinity (Mundell-Fleming trilemma) states that a country cannot simultaneously maintain (1) a fixed exchange rate, (2) free capital mobility, and (3) independent monetary policy. It must sacrifice one. For example, China historically maintained a fixed exchange rate and independent monetary policy by restricting capital flows. The eurozone countries sacrifice independent monetary policy to maintain fixed exchange rates (the euro) and free capital mobility within the EU. The US sacrifices exchange rate stability to maintain monetary independence and capital mobility.

rubric.key: Explains why all three cannot coexist

questions.question: What is a global value chain and how has it changed the nature of international trade? Explain one benefit and one risk for developing countries that participate in GVCs.
questions.answer: A global value chain is the set of activities required to bring a product from conception to end use, now fragmented across multiple countries. Rather than trading finished goods, countries now trade tasks and components. Benefit: developing countries can industrialize by joining a GVC at a low-skill stage (e.g., assembly) without building an entire industry from scratch, gaining jobs, technology transfer, and export revenue. Risk: countries can become trapped in low-value activities (e.g., assembly only) with thin margins, vulnerability to MNC relocation, and limited technology upgrading.

rubric.key: Defines GVC and explains trade transformation
Identifies a clear benefit for developing countries
Identifies a clear risk for developing countries

questions.question: Summarize the debate between Jeffrey Sachs and William Easterly on foreign aid. What is the core disagreement and which argument do you find more persuasive, and why?
questions.answer: Sachs argues that extremely poor countries are caught in poverty traps and need a 'big push' of massive, coordinated aid to escape. He advocates scaling up aid to meet the Millennium/Sustainable Development Goals. Easterly counters that top-down aid fails because it ignores local knowledge, creates perverse incentives, empowers corrupt governments, and lacks accountability. He favors bottom-up, market-based approaches and searchers over planners. The core disagreement is whether poverty is primarily a resource problem (Sachs) or an institutional/incentive problem (Easterly). Student evaluation should be supported by reasoning and evidence.

major_assignments.title: International Economic Policy Analysis
major_assignments.description: Select a major international economic policy issue (trade dispute, financial crisis, development program, or currency regime) and produce a comprehensive analysis that integrates economic theory with political context.
objectives: Apply international economic theory to a real policy case
Analyze the political economy dimensions of economic policy
Evaluate competing policy approaches with evidence
deliverables: Data appendix with relevant economic indicators

criteria: Appropriate use of economic data and indicators
Integration of political and economic analysis
Rigorous integration of economic theory and political context
Strong evidence base with appropriate data analysis

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on economic concepts and current events

participation.description: Engagement in discussions, simulations, and data exercises

major_assignments.description: International economic policy analysis paper and presentation

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering trade, finance, and development theory

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam integrating all economic topics with policy analysis

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. More than 3 unexcused absences reduce the participation grade to zero.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late papers penalized 5% per day. Data appendices cannot be submitted late.
course_policies.collaboration: Study groups and data analysis collaboration encouraged. All written analysis must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citation. Fabricating data results in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to economic databases (World Bank, IMF, WTO), spreadsheet software, and course LMS.
